module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
sublecture:
2.2.2 Manifesto of the Communist Party, 1848 - Part 2

content: Who are chartered burghers? So in medieval Europe, many towns bought or won charters. So these were legal documents granting them certain rights and privileges, such as self-rule exemption from feudal duties and ability to trade freely. Because remember at the time, you can't just trade with whoever you want because you have to get permission from the queen or the king or whoever, right. So these chartered burghers where these small townships where they sort of got the right to be able to, within that area, you can trade freely. So these chartered towns became centers of commerce and craft production. Their residence, the chartered burghers were no longer serfs tied to the land, but free citizens engaged in trade and manufacture. So the middle ages the system from there, they were able to sort of eke out these small chartered towns. So you had the serfs. Who are peasants bound to the land from there, you had some form of small traders. Chartered burghers you see in medieval Europe, but in other places they were called differently the, you know, the exact terms of what they could do and could not do, might have been different, but basically they were some type of tradespeople. Now as these tradespeople kept accumulating capital and kept producing more and more, their ability to produce became more and more as the instruments of production became better. That's when you see the bourgeoisie, which is the capitalist class. So what Marx is saying is that this has sort of an inevitability to it. As the modes of production, the instruments of production become better and better, and the relations of production have to change and when that changes the social structure, the political system, the value system, everything has to change. So according to Marx, the discovery of America, access to the East Indian Chinese markets, colonization, trade with the colonies, increase in the means of exchange and commodities led to more and more commerce and industry, which is the revolutionary element which destroyed feudal society. So Marx has a very economic perspective on things. He is not saying that ideology is what changed the world. He is saying it's economics, so everything including colonization, globalization, all of this is because means of production became better and better. So markets had to keep increasing and so you had to colonize, you had to globalize and which is what killed the feudal system, the old aristocratic system. So why is it inevitable? Because the feudal system where industrial production was monopolized by these closed guilds was no longer sufficient to take care of the growing needs of the new markets. It was replaced by the manufacturing system and the feudal system of aristocrats and guage were pushed aside because of this rise of this capitalist manufacturing class, which could produce more and more. They just couldn't compete these small guilds. So the division of labor was no longer between aristocrats and various. You do trade. I am I will do scholarship. You are the you know the warrior class, I am the scholarly class. That's not the division of labor. The division of labor is on the factory floor. So that was the change that happened. But it didn't stop there. So what Marx is saying is that once these forces of production get going, nobody can stop it. Because markets kept growing, demand kept rising. Even manufacturing was not enough and then you had steam engine machinery, bigger and bigger machinery. It kept revolutionizing industrial production. And so the manufacturing system got superseded by this giant modern industry, which is driven by these industrial millionaires leading large industrial armies. So as modern industries started taking over more and more, it pushed into the background. All other classes handed down from the middle ages. So no longer do you have like 4 different classes. You just have bourgeoisie and proletariat. So the bourgeoisie, the cap capitalism, according to Marx, is a result of a long course of development of a series of revolutions in the modes of production and exchange. And each advance of the bourgeoisie was accompanied by increasing advances in their political power. At first, the capitalist was an oppressed class under the sway of the feudal nobility. So the aristocrats, the capitalists, the small trader was actually an oppressed class. They will looked down upon. They are money makers, they are greedy. So there was all this moral injunctions against, you know, doing all this commerce is considered somehow lower. Moneymaking is considered somehow lesser. The feudal nobility was at the top, so that's how they started. They started as an oppressed class, the bourgeoisie, and then they became a self-governing association where later they started arming themselves. The exact form of this kind of self-governing association was different so it was an independent of Republic in Italy and Germany. While in France it was a taxable third estate of the monarchy. So you go from the capitalist being an oppressed class to being some kind of, okay, they are there, they are important, but they are not the most important. But then once manufacturing took off, the bourgeoisie became a counter power serving the monarchy against the nobles. And now Marx claims with the establishment of modern industry, bourgeoisie has taken over exclusive political power. So Marx says that, 'The modern state is, but a committee for managing the common affairs of the whole bourgeoisie'. So, so today, according to Marx, and he's talking in 1848, he's saying that the modern state is nothing but spokesperson for the capitalist. So Marx says that, 'The bourgeoisie, wherever it has not the upper hand, has put an end to all feudal, patriarchal idyllic relations. It has pitilessly torn asunder the motley feudal ties that bound man to his 'natural superiors' and has left remaining. No other nexus between man and man than naked self-interest, than callous 'cash payment'. It has drowned the most heavenly ecstasies of religious fervour, of chivalrous enthusiasm, of philistine sentimentalism, in the icy water of egotistical calculation'. If you have time, you know, read the original manuscript. Marx writes very well. I gave you a small quote and we will I'll quote Marx thorughout but the, but I hope you can appreciate the beauty of the language itself. So Marx continues the same quote and says, 'It has resolved personal worth into exchange value, and in place of the numberless indefensible chartered freedoms, has set up that single unconscionable freedom - Free Trade. In one word for exploitation, wailed by religious and political illusions, it has substituted naked, shameless, direct, brutal exploitation'. I mean, this is, you know, you don't have to agree with Marx, but you have to be able to appreciate the beauty of his language. It's not, it's not completely surprising how he was able to win over a large portion of the world in his time. So what is Marx saying here? Marx is basically saying that, you know, all these illusions that we had about loyalty, serving the feudal Lord, putting down your life for the feudal, Lord, religious fervour the, you know, the church is somehow speaking God's word all of that we left behind. And now our value systems are based on greed, on self-interest. So the only relations between human and human is callous, cash payment, self-interest, all of that religious fervour, loyalty, all of that is gone. All of those ideals. So this is, you know, you can see the idea that we discussed before about base and superstructure, how Marx is saying how all of this religious stuff, culture, art, all of this is it's just there to support the base, which is the forces of production. So Marx goes on to see how this constant search for new markets and new products which it continuously revolutionizes the instruments of production. So because you are constantly competing, constantly searching for new markets and new products, there is this constant innovation happening. Constant revolutionizing of production, uninterrupted disturbance of all social conditions, everlasting uncertainty and agitation distinguished the bourgeoisie epoch from all earlier ones. All fixed, fast-frozen relations, with their train of ancient and venerable prejudices and opinions, are swept away, all newly formed ideas become antiquated before they can ossify. All that is solid melts into air. All that is holy is profaned, and man is at last compelled to face with sober senses, his real conditions of life and his relations with his kind'. So again, Marx is talking about how all those fixed ideas the feudal ideas, monarchy, aristocracy, all of that, all of those venerable prejudices, all of that has been swept away and goes on to say the need of a constantly expanding market for its products chases the bourgeoisie all over the whole surface of the globe. It must nestle everywhere, settle everywhere, establish connections everywhere, so this is essentially how Marx is explaining colonialism and globalization. So Marx goes on to say the bourgeoisie has through its exploitation of the world market, given a cosmopolitan character to production and consumption in every country to the great chagrin of reactionists, it has drawn from under the feet of industry, the national ground on which it stood. All old established national industries have been destroyed or are daily being destroyed. They are dislodged by new industries whose introduction becomes a life and death question for all civilized nations by industries that no longer work up indigenous raw material, but raw material drawn from the remotest zones, industries whose products are consumed not only at home, but in every quarter of the globe in place of the old wants satisfied by the production of the country, we find wants requiring for their satisfaction in the products of distant lands and climbs. In place of the old, local and national seclusion and self-sufficiency, we have intercourse in every direction. Universal interdependence of nations, and as in material, so also in intellectual production. The intellectual creations of individual nations become common property. National one sidedness and narrow-mindedness become more and more impossible and from the numerous national and local literatures there arises of world literature. Yeah, so this is what Marx is saying. He's explaining standardization, globalization across the world. You go to any country, you see a McDonald's and a Burger King, and you know, the processes are very similar. Now, wherever you go, there is a huge mall wherever you go, the, you know, the systems look more and more similar. This is exactly what Marx is saying, that capitalism forces this kind of standardization because people want access to products and goods from all over the world. And, and so you have these huge production machinery supplying the globe. And so Marx says, how the bourgeoisie by the rapid improvement of all instruments of production by the immensely facilitated means of communication draws all, even the most barbarian nations into civilization. The cheap prices of its commodities are the heavy artillery with which it bats down all Chinese walls with which it forces the barbarians, intensely obstinate hatred of foreigners to capitulate. It compels all nations on pain of extinction to adopt the bourgeoisie mode of production. It compels them to introduce what it calls civilization into their midst that is to become bourgeoisie themselves in one word. It creates a world after its own image. Remember this last phrase you know, appreciate the rest of it, but remember this last phrase, you'll see echoes of this phrase coming up in various literature that you read, yeah. In one word it creates a word after its own image. So this is what Marx is saying that it's not, globalization is not just about goods and it's also about culture. It's becoming more and more standardized. And so Marx is saying that this kind of civilization, what we are calling civilization it's battering down. It's the artillery that is battering down all the Chinese walls, all the attempts to keep ourselves separate and different. And so it creates these enormous cities because you need more and more workers working in these huge factories. So your people are coming together to and creating these enormous cities. So you have greatly increased urban pollution as compared to rural. And there is education because you need these workers to be able to work in these massive fab factories. Marx is describing how all of these changes can be explained by changes in the material, that is, the economic conditions, changes in the forces of production, the mode of production and exchange. This, according to Marx, is it's inexorable this change. Nobody can stop it. Nobody's making it happen. It is because the economic conditions are changing that all of these relations of production are changing. And so you have, you see a change in value systems. You see globalization, you see urbanization, all of the changes that frankly we see everywhere around us. And so with that, I will end this subsection. And in the subsection, we have essentially seen Marx's explanation of 2 of the main concepts that for us as social scientists are very interesting. The idea of hi historical materialism, that is, history evolves due to the material that's the reason for the word historical materialism. History changes because the materials change the economic because of the economic changes. Yeah, and because the economic change happens in the modes of production, then you have a change in the relations of production between the classes, and that then changes the superstructure. All the stuff on top, according to Marx, the ideology, the morals, the religion, the art, the literature, all of that changes to be, to coincide and to be consistent with the new modes of production and exchange. So with that, I'll end this clip, and then in the next clip we will look at Marx's ideas for why, you know, inevitably now this downfall has to happen in capitalism.
